title: Sampling Distribution of the Sample Mean

epigraph.text: A faithful witness does not lie, but a false witness breathes out lies.
epigraph.quote: The sample mean is the best estimator of the population mean.

content.text:
The sample mean $\xbar$ is the most common statistic. Understanding 
its sampling distribution is fundamental to inference.

content.title: Mean and Standard Error of $\xbar$
content.content:
If $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ are a random sample from a population 
with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, then:
\[
 \E[\xbar] = \mu \qquad \text{and} \qquad \SD(\xbar) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$\xbar = \frac{1}{n}(X_1 + \cdots + X_n)$.

By linearity: $\E[\xbar] = \frac{1}{n}(n\mu) = \mu$.

By independence: $\Var(\xbar) = \frac{1}{n^2}(n\sigma^2) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$.

Therefore $\SD(\xbar) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$.

content.title: Standard Error
content.content:
The **standard error** of a statistic is 
the standard deviation of its sampling distribution.

For the sample mean: $\SE(\xbar) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$.

content.title: $\SE$ vs.\ $\SD$
content.content:
Standard deviation ($\SD$) measures the spread of individual 
observations. Standard error ($\SE$) measures the spread of a 
statistic across samples. They are related by $\SE(\xbar) = \frac{\SD}{\sqrt{n}}$.

content.text: Larger samples give more precise estimates.

content.title: Standard Error
content.problem:
A population has $\mu = 100$ and $\sigma = 15$. Find $\SE(\xbar)$ for:

1. $n = 9$

2. $n = 36$

3. $n = 225$

content.title: Sampling from Normal Distribution
content.content:
If $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ are a random sample from $\Normal(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then:
\[
 \xbar \sim \Normal\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)
\]
Equivalently:
\[
 Z = \frac{\xbar - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \sim \Normal(0, 1)
\]
content.label: Theorem
